message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-2; pass 1 already merged 22 fixes). Venv (run PoCs): /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
Dataset-agnostic ML/methodology engine being hardened for PRODUCTION. Hard constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only), no metered API (subscription OAuth), engine imports no adapter/dataset (ADR-0002), optional heavy backends lazy-imported (ADR-0006). Read real code; back findings with a concrete repro/PoC. Report ONLY genuine production-readiness defects, not style. Pass-1 findings #101-#122 are already FIXED — do not re-report them; DO report anything they missed or any regression they introduced.

Independent VERIFIER #2 of 2. A pass-2 assessor reported:
TITLE: Inconsistent @runtime_checkable usage: seam Protocols give name-only validation; adapter contract Protocols give none
DIMENSION: public API & adapter-Protocol stability
SEVERITY: MEDIUM
FILE: src/assay_engine/contracts/parser.py:16; src/assay_engine/contracts/claims.py:42; src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:33; src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:43; src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py:24; src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:172 
DESCRIPTION: The seam Protocols (DataVersioner, Tracer, ExperimentTracker, VectorStore, the reasoning seam) are decorated @runtime_checkable, but the core engine↔adapter contract Protocols a clone must implement — IngestionParser, ExternalClaimsSource, FeatureBuilder, BaselineBuilder, TimestampAuthority, HeldOutConfirmer, ClaimConfirmer — are NOT. This is a stability inconsistency with two distinct hazards: (1) isinstance against the adapter Protocols raises TypeError ('Instance and class checks can only be used with @runtime_checkable protocols'), so neither the engine nor a clone can defensively isinstance-check an adapter object — PoC verified. (2) The @runtime_checkable that DOES exist is a false-confidence trap: it validates method NAMES only, not signatures, so isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner) returns True even when put()/fingerprint() take the wrong arguments (PoC verified). Meanwhile none of the @runtime_checkable seam Protocols are actually isinstance-checked anywhere in src/, so the decorator is currently dead weight that invites callers (and clones) to rely on a check that wouldn't catch a real misimplementation.
SUGGESTED FIX: Pick one consistent policy. Recommended: keep adapter validation behavior-based (call the methods, normalize failures to typed errors as the parser/Verdict fixes above do) and DROP the @runtime_checkable decorators that are never used, OR document explicitly that runtime_checkable is name-only and is not a substitute for signature/behavior validation. If isinstance-based adapter validation is desired, make the contract Protocols runtime_checkable too — but pair it with explicit call-time checks since names-only isinstance will not catch wrong signatures/return types.
Independently determine if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem. Read the code; PoC where applicable. is_real=false if false positive / already handled / documented-justified scope. Recalibrate severity. Give evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Ingestion parser interface — the only adapter piece that understands the raw format.
2	
3	An adapter implements :class:`IngestionParser` to turn its native source into a canonical
4	:class:`~assay_engine.contracts.schema.Corpus`. The engine calls ``parse`` and works only
5	with the canonical result.
6	"""
7	
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	from pathlib import Path
11	from typing import Protocol
12	
13	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
14	
15	
16	class IngestionParser(Protocol):
17	    """Raw source → canonical :class:`Corpus`.
18	
19	    Implementations live in adapters. They must be deterministic: the same source bytes
20	    produce the same ``Corpus`` (so the baseline is reproducible). Implementations must not
21	    read any external-claims artifact here — claims load through a separate provider.
22	    """
23	
24	    def parse(self, source: Path) -> Corpus:
25	        """Parse ``source`` into a canonical corpus."""
26	        ...
27	
28	    def source_fingerprint(self, source: Path) -> str:
29	        """Return a stable content hash of ``source`` for provenance and determinism checks."""
30	        ...
31

1	"""External-claims source interface — optional, and quarantined from the baseline.
2	
3	If a dataset ships claims asserted by some external authority (relationships, labels, a
4	taxonomy, mappings), an adapter exposes them through :class:`ExternalClaimsSource`. The
5	engine converts each :class:`ClaimRecord` into a typed, falsifiable hypothesis and tests it
6	against the baseline — which is kept blind to these claims (Firewall A).
7	
8	This interface is *structurally separable*: the baseline pipeline never receives it, so the
9	baseline can be produced with claims withheld. Pure-discovery datasets omit it entirely.
10	Access to a claims source while claim-blindness is in force raises ``FirewallViolation``
11	(see :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.firewalls`).
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Iterable, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	
21	
22	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
23	class ClaimRecord:
24	    """One external, expert-asserted claim about the data — a falsifiable assertion.
25	
26	    The engine treats this as a *hypothesis source*, never as ground truth. ``subject`` and
27	    ``referents`` name the units the claim is about; ``assertion`` describes what is claimed
28	    in a typed, machine-readable way; ``provenance`` records who asserted it and where from.
29	    """
30	
31	    claim_id: str
32	    subject: str
33	    referents: tuple[str, ...]
34	    assertion: Mapping[str, Any]
35	    provenance: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
36	
37	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
38	        object.__setattr__(self, "assertion", freeze_mapping(self.assertion))
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "provenance", freeze_mapping(self.provenance))
40	
41	
42	class ExternalClaimsSource(Protocol):
43	    """Adapter-provided access to a dataset's external claims (adjudication mode only)."""
44	
45	    def claims(self) -> Iterable[ClaimRecord]:
46	        """Yield every external claim to be adjudicated against the blind baseline."""
47	        ...
48	
49	    def claim_fingerprint(self) -> str:
50	        """Stable content hash of the claim set, for provenance and pre-registration."""
51	        ...
52

1	"""Feature-builder interface — optional dataset-specific features.
2	
3	The baseline toolkit provides generic builders (embeddings, similarity, etc.). When a
4	dataset has features only meaningful in its own domain, an adapter supplies them through a
5	:class:`FeatureBuilder`. Output is a plain numeric :class:`FeatureMatrix` keyed by unit id,
6	so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Protocol, Sequence
13	
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
15	
16	
17	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
18	class FeatureMatrix:
19	    """Numeric features for a set of units. ``rows[i]`` corresponds to ``unit_ids[i]``."""
20	
21	    unit_ids: tuple[str, ...]
22	    feature_names: tuple[str, ...]
23	    rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...]
24	
25	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
26	        if len(self.rows) != len(self.unit_ids):
27	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix row count must match unit_ids length")
28	        width = len(self.feature_names)
29	        if any(len(r) != width for r in self.rows):
30	            raise ValueError("FeatureMatrix rows must match feature_names width")
31	
32	
33	class FeatureBuilder(Protocol):
34	    """Build dataset-specific numeric features from a canonical corpus."""
35	
36	    def build(self, corpus: Corpus) -> FeatureMatrix:
37	        """Compute features for (a subset of) the corpus units. Must be deterministic."""
38	        ...
39	
40	    @property
41	    def provides(self) -> Sequence[str]:
42	        """Names of the features this builder produces (for provenance)."""
43	        ...
44

1	"""Baseline builder contract + a deterministic artifact wrapper.
2	
3	This module fixes the *shape* of a baseline artifact and the build contract. The engine ships
4	the dataset-agnostic building blocks for a baseline (similarity/distance, descriptive stats,
5	the determinism harness in :mod:`assay_engine.baseline`); the choice-bearing builder that
6	assembles them — which embedding model, which clustering, which graph — encodes dataset
7	decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter as a :class:`BaselineBuilder` (ADR-0002).
8	
9	Firewall A is enforced here structurally: :meth:`BaselineBuilder.build` accepts a
10	:class:`~assay_engine.methodology.firewalls.ClaimBlindGuard` and never accepts a claims
11	source — a baseline simply cannot be handed the claims it must stay blind to.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
17	from typing import Any, Mapping, Protocol
18	
19	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
20	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ExternalClaimsSource
21	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
22	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard
23	
24	
25	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
26	class BaselineArtifact:
27	    """A deterministic, versioned empirical model of the corpus — the one privileged object.
28	
29	    Concrete contents (embedding matrices, similarity graphs, cluster assignments, etc.) live
30	    under ``contents`` keyed by builder name. ``determinism`` records the seeds, input hashes,
31	    and model/version strings needed to reproduce it.
32	    """
33	
34	    corpus_fingerprint: str
35	    contents: Mapping[str, Any]
36	    determinism: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
37	
38	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
39	        object.__setattr__(self, "contents", freeze_mapping(self.contents))
40	        object.__setattr__(self, "determinism", freeze_mapping(self.determinism))
41	
42	
43	class BaselineBuilder(Protocol):
44	    """Build a :class:`BaselineArtifact` from a corpus, blind to any external claims."""
45	
46	    def build(
47	        self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource]
48	    ) -> BaselineArtifact:
49	        """Construct the baseline. Must run with ``claim_guard.sealed()`` in force."""
50	        ...
51

1	"""Data versioning — content-addressed, local, deterministic (METHODOLOGY.md §7, ADR-0003).
2	
3	Every finding must cite the exact bytes it was computed from. This provides a dependency-free,
4	on-box content-addressing store: an artifact is hashed (SHA-256) and copied under a local
5	store keyed by that hash, so the same bytes always yield the same version id and any artifact
6	can be re-fetched by id. (The prior platform used DVC via an external tool; this keeps the
7	engine self-contained — an adapter may swap in DVC by implementing the same Protocol.)
8	"""
9	
10	from __future__ import annotations
11	
12	import hashlib
13	import os
14	import re
15	import shutil
16	import tempfile
17	from pathlib import Path
18	from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable
19	
20	_CHUNK = 1 << 20  # 1 MiB
21	_SHA256_HEX = re.compile(r"[0-9a-f]{64}")
22	
23	
24	@runtime_checkable
25	class DataVersioner(Protocol):
26	    def put(self, path: str) -> str:
27	        """Version the artifact at ``path``; return its content hash / version id."""
28	        ...
29	
30	    def fingerprint(self, path: str) -> str:
31	        """Return the content hash of ``path`` without storing it."""
32	        ...
33	
34	
35	def _hash_file(path: Path) -> str:
36	    h = hashlib.sha256()
37	    with path.open("rb") as f:
38	        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(_CHUNK), b""):
39	            h.update(chunk)
40	    return h.hexdigest()
41	
42	
43	class LocalDataVersioner:
44	    """Content-addressed local artifact store. Deterministic and fully offline.
45	
46	    ``store_dir`` defaults to ``ASSAY_DATA_STORE`` or ``./.assay-data``. Files are stored under
47	    ``<store>/<aa>/<full-hash>`` (sharded by the first two hex chars).
48	    """
49	
50	    def __init__(self, store_dir: str | None = None) -> None:
51	        root = store_dir or os.environ.get("ASSAY_DATA_STORE") or str(Path.cwd() / ".assay-data")
52	        self._root = Path(root)
53	
54	    def fingerprint(self, path: str) -> str:
55	        p = Path(path)
56	        if not p.is_file():
57	            raise FileNotFoundError(f"cannot version a non-file path: {path!r}")
58	        return _hash_file(p)
59	
60	    def put(self, path: str) -> str:
61	        """Store the artifact under its content hash; return the hash (version id).
62	
63	        Idempotent: storing the same bytes twice is a no-op returning the same id. Publish is
64	        atomic — content is copied to a *per-writer-unique* temp file (so two processes
65	        storing the same digest never clobber a shared temp) and then ``os.replace``-d into
66	        place, so a reader never observes a partial artifact. The store is content-addressed,
67	        so an interrupted/duplicate write is harmless.
68	        """
69	        digest = self.fingerprint(path)
70	        dest = self._root / digest[:2] / digest
71	        if dest.exists():
72	            return digest
73	        dest.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
74	        fd, tmp_name = tempfile.mkstemp(dir=str(dest.parent), prefix=".tmp-")
75	        os.close(fd)
76	        tmp = Path(tmp_name)
77	        try:
78	            shutil.copy2(path, tmp)
79	            os.replace(tmp, dest)  # atomic publish within the same filesystem
80	        finally:
81	            tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True)  # no-op once replaced; cleans up on copy failure
82	        return digest
83	
84	    def path_for(self, digest: str) -> Path:
85	        """Local path of a previously-stored artifact by its version id.
86	
87	        The version id MUST be a SHA-256 hex digest (the only form :meth:`put` ever returns).
88	        Validating it rejects path-traversal payloads (e.g. ``../../etc/passwd``) that would
89	        otherwise compose a path outside the store (#112).
90	        """
91	        if not _SHA256_HEX.fullmatch(digest):
92	            raise ValueError(
93	                f"invalid version id {digest!r} (expected a 64-char sha256 hex digest)"
94	            )
95	        return self._root / digest[:2] / digest
96

159	    token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("assay.run_id", run_id))
160	    try:
161	        yield
162	    finally:
163	        context.detach(token)
164	
165	
166	# OpenInference requires every span carry a span kind so Phoenix classifies it (CHAIN/LLM/
167	# AGENT/TOOL); a plain OTel span with no kind renders as UNKNOWN in the Phoenix UI. We set the
168	# raw semantic-convention attribute directly (no openinference import needed for a manual span).
169	_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND = "openinference.span.kind"
170	
171	
172	@runtime_checkable
173	class Tracer(Protocol):
174	    @contextmanager
175	    def span(
176	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
177	    ) -> Iterator[None]: ...
178	
179	
180	class OtelTracer:
181	    """A tracer that emits OpenTelemetry spans via the global provider (no-op if OTel absent).
182	
183	    Use this where a component wants explicit spans rather than relying on auto-instrumentation;
184	    it honors whatever provider :func:`bootstrap_tracing` registered. Each span is stamped with
185	    its OpenInference ``kind`` so Phoenix classifies it (default ``CHAIN``).
186	    """
187	
188	    @contextmanager
189	    def span(
190	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
191	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
192	        try:
193	            from opentelemetry import trace
194	        except ImportError:
195	            yield
196	            return
197	        tracer = trace.get_tracer("assay_engine")
198	        with tracer.start_as_current_span(name) as sp:
199	            sp.set_attribute(_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, kind)
200	            for k, v in (attributes or {}).items():
201	                sp.set_attribute(k, v)
202	            yield
203	
204	
205	class UnconfiguredTracer:
206	    """Fail-loud placeholder for contexts that must not silently drop spans."""
207	
208	    @contextmanager
209	    def span(

content.text:
The claim's two factual sub-claims appear correct so far:
1. Seam Protocols are `@runtime_checkable`, adapter contract Protocols are not.
2. None of the `@runtime_checkable` seam Protocols are isinstance-checked anywhere in src/ (the isinstance hits are all on SDK message types, not on the Protocols).

Let me check the seam.py runtime_checkable usage and look for any isinstance usage in tests, plus verify the reasoning seam Protocol.

533	# Public seam

536	class ReasoningSeam(Protocol):
537	    """Route a reasoning request to the appropriate tier with timeouts, retries, tracing."""

542	@contextmanager

544	    """Emit an OpenTelemetry span if a provider is registered; a cheap no-op otherwise.
545	
546	    Stamps the OpenInference ``openinference.span.kind`` (default ``AGENT`` — a reasoning call)
547	    so Phoenix classifies the span instead of rendering it UNKNOWN. OpenTelemetry is imported
548	    lazily, so the seam traces when the (self-hosted, on-box) observability stack is wired and is
549	    a silent no-op in tests / offline (ADR-0003).
550	    """
551	    try:
552	        from opentelemetry import trace
553	    except ImportError:
554	        yield
555	        return
556	    tracer = trace.get_tracer("assay_engine.reasoning")
557	    with tracer.start_as_current_span(name) as span:

content.text: The seam Protocols are used purely as static type annotations (`tracer: Tracer | None = None`, etc.), never isinstance-checked. The decorator is indeed unused at runtime. Now let me run the PoC to verify both hazards concretely.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && .venv/bin/python - <<'PY'
from assay_engine.persistence.versioning import DataVersioner
from assay_engine.contracts.parser import IngestionParser

# Hazard 2: name-only isinstance returns True for wrong signatures
class WrongVersioner:
    def put(self, a, b, c):      # wrong arity
        return "x"
    def fingerprint(self):       # wrong arity (no path)
        return "y"

wv = WrongVersioner()
print("WrongVersioner isinstance DataVersioner:", isinstance(wv, DataVersioner))

# Hazard: missing a method -> False (name-only does catch missing names)
class MissingMethod:
    def put(self, path): return "x"
print("MissingMethod (no fingerprint) isinstance DataVersioner:", isinstance(MissingMethod(), DataVersioner))

# Hazard 1: isinstance against non-runtime_checkable adapter Protocol raises TypeError
class SomeParser:
    def parse(self, source): ...
    def source_fingerprint(self, source): return "z"
try:
    print("isinstance against IngestionParser:", isinstance(SomeParser(), IngestionParser))
except TypeError as e:
    print("TypeError on IngestionParser isinstance:", str(e)[:90])
PY

content.content:
WrongVersioner isinstance DataVersioner: True
MissingMethod (no fingerprint) isinstance DataVersioner: False
TypeError on IngestionParser isinstance: Instance and class checks can only be used with @runtime_checkable protocols

content.text:
Both PoCs are confirmed exactly as the assessor described:
- `isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner)` returns `True` despite wrong signatures (name-only validation).
- `isinstance(SomeParser(), IngestionParser)` raises `TypeError` (not runtime_checkable).

Now the key question for production-readiness: **is this actually a defect that can cause a real failure**, or is it a documented/inconsequential inconsistency? The decisive fact is whether anything in the codebase actually relies on these isinstance checks. I already confirmed nothing in src/ does. Let me also confirm there's no plugin/factory loader that does duck-type validation expecting isinstance, and check whether public API re-exports these as a validation surface.

- No documentation mentions `runtime_checkable` or its name-only limitation.

content.text:
The `@runtime_checkable` decorators predate pass 1 (added at the engine scaffold). Not a regression. Pass 1 did not touch this inconsistency.

Now to recalibrate severity. Let me assess the actual production impact honestly:

The factual claims are all verified. But the severity hinges on real-world consequence:

1. **TypeError on adapter Protocols** — this only triggers if someone actually *writes* `isinstance(x, IngestionParser)`. Nothing in the engine does, and a clone author who tries it gets an immediate, loud, self-explanatory `TypeError` at the line they wrote — not a silent production failure. This is a fail-loud condition, not a latent defect.

2. **Name-only false-confidence on seam Protocols** — this is the more substantive concern: the decorator is present, advertises an isinstance capability, and that capability silently accepts wrong-signature implementations. But again: nothing in src/ relies on it. It is dead weight that *invites* a future misuse, not an active defect.

This is a genuine API-hygiene/stability inconsistency, real and PoC-verified, but it causes **no incorrect behavior in the current engine** — no engine code path or test depends on these checks, so no run produces a wrong result, crash, or data issue today. The risk is purely latent/foot-gun for a future clone author. That places it below MEDIUM (which the assessor assigned). It's a real, fixable LOW — a polish/consistency item, not a production-readiness blocker.

input.evidence:
All factual sub-claims PoC-verified against real code.

(1) Decorator inventory (grep src/): @runtime_checkable is on the SEAM Protocols only — DataVersioner (persistence/versioning.py:24), Tracer (observability/tracing.py:172), ExperimentTracker (observability/tracking.py:33), VectorStore (persistence/vectorstore.py:24), ReasoningSeam (reasoning/seam.py:535). The adapter/methodology contract Protocols are NOT decorated: IngestionParser (contracts/parser.py:16), ExternalClaimsSource (contracts/claims.py:42), FeatureBuilder (contracts/features.py:33), BaselineBuilder (baseline/toolkit.py:43), TimestampAuthority (methodology/preregistration.py:71), HeldOutConfirmer (methodology/discovery.py:42), ClaimConfirmer (methodology/adjudication.py:43).

(2) PoC (.venv/bin/python), both hazards reproduce exactly:
  - isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner) -> True  (put(self,a,b,c) and fingerprint(self) — wrong arity — still pass; name-only)
  - isinstance(MissingMethod(), DataVersioner) -> False  (missing name IS caught)
  - isinstance(SomeParser(), IngestionParser) -> TypeError: "Instance and class checks can only be used with @runtime_checkable protocols"

(3) Dead-weight claim verified: grep for isinstance in src/ shows the ONLY isinstance calls are in reasoning/seam.py (lines 346/408/410/412) against SDK message types (AssistantMessage/TextBlock/ResultMessage), NOT against any of the @runtime_checkable Protocols. No conforms/validate_adapter/check_protocol helper exists. Seam Protocols are used purely as static annotations (pipeline.py: `tracer: Tracer | None`, `versioner: DataVersioner | None`, etc.). So the @runtime_checkable capability is never exercised anywhere in the engine.

(4) Not a regression / not already fixed: git log -S shows the decorators date to the original engine scaffold (2c210f9), untouched by pass-1 (#123). No docs mention runtime_checkable or the name-only limitation (grep docs/ *.md empty).
input.reasoning:
The inconsistency is real and accurately described; every sub-claim reproduces. However, I am recalibrating severity DOWN from the reported MEDIUM to LOW, because the defect causes no incorrect behavior in the current engine and is not a production-readiness blocker — it is a latent API-hygiene/foot-gun.

Why LOW, not MEDIUM:
- No code path or test depends on these isinstance checks (verified: zero isinstance against any of these Protocols anywhere in src/ or tests/). Therefore today no engine run can crash, return a wrong result, or corrupt data because of this. The reported "hazards" are both potential-future-misuse, not active failures.
- Hazard 1 (TypeError on adapter Protocols) is fail-loud: it only fires if a clone author literally writes `isinstance(x, IngestionParser)`, and they get an immediate, self-explanatory TypeError on their own line — not a silent production fault. A loud, deterministic error at authoring time is low-impact.
- Hazard 2 (name-only false confidence) is the more legitimate concern — the decorator advertises a validation capability that silently accepts wrong-signature impls — but since nothing relies on it, it is dead weight that *invites* misuse rather than constituting one. That is a polish/consistency defect.

It is genuinely worth fixing for a "production" public engine (clones rely on contract clarity), and the assessor's suggested fix is sound: pick one policy — drop the unused @runtime_checkable decorators (they are never exercised) OR document explicitly that they are name-only and not a behavior check; if isinstance validation is actually wanted, make it consistent across all contract Protocols AND pair it with explicit call-time/signature checks since names-only will not catch wrong signatures. But the absence of any real failure mode keeps this below MEDIUM.

content.text:
I verified the pass-2 assessor's finding independently. My conclusion:

**is_real = true, but severity recalibrated from MEDIUM to LOW.**

All factual sub-claims reproduce exactly (PoC run with the project venv):
- `isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner)` → `True` even with wrong-arity `put`/`fingerprint` (name-only validation).
- `isinstance(SomeParser(), IngestionParser)` → `TypeError` (adapter contract Protocols are not `@runtime_checkable`).
- The `@runtime_checkable` decorators on the seam Protocols are dead weight: the only `isinstance` calls in `src/` are in `reasoning/seam.py` against SDK message types, never against any of these Protocols. No `conforms`/`validate_adapter` helper exists; the Protocols are used purely as static annotations.

It is not a regression and was not previously fixed — the decorators date to the original engine scaffold (commit `2c210f9`), untouched by pass 1.

Why LOW rather than MEDIUM: nothing in the engine or tests depends on these isinstance checks, so today there is no code path that crashes, returns wrong results, or corrupts data because of this. Hazard 1 is fail-loud (immediate, self-explanatory TypeError on the author's own line). Hazard 2 (name-only false confidence) is the more legitimate concern but is a latent foot-gun, not an active failure. It is worth fixing for a public engine — drop the unused decorators or document the name-only limitation, and if isinstance-based validation is actually wanted, make it consistent across all contract Protocols and pair it with explicit call-time signature checks — but the absence of any real failure mode keeps it below MEDIUM.

Relevant files: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/versioning.py:24`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:172`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracking.py:33`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py:24`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:535`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/contracts/parser.py:16`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/contracts/claims.py:42`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/contracts/features.py:33`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/toolkit.py:43`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:71`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:42`, `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:43`.
